classification.summary: Introductory post announcing the purpose of a new blog as a content manager for a web design and martial arts portfolio site. The author outlines plans to cover web design, social networking, health and fitness, and martial arts.

A guide to legal and policy considerations around social media networking for businesses, covering ISP/ICP distinctions, defamation, FOIA, and terms of service. Practical bullet-point notes on liability and content responsibility for organizations using online platforms.

An introduction to Eastern philosophies drawn from the author's martial arts journey since 1994, exploring Taoism, Buddhism, Confucianism, and related traditions. The post reflects on how studying Korean and Japanese arts opened a window into distinctly non-Western ways of seeing language and the world.

Social media participation requires careful attention to intellectual property, citation, and reputation. Prudent linking and attribution benefit both creators and readers while avoiding defamation risks.
A brief site update listing planned improvements to web design, mobile interface, and backend code. No theological or scriptural content is present.

title: Perceptions of Advanced Capitalism and Corporation Influence upon American Middle Class and Individual Political Efficacy in Post-Modern America

classification.summary: A request for dissertation research participation studying perceptions of corporate power and its influence on public policy and individual political efficacy. The author invites colleagues and networks to complete an anonymous online survey on the topic.

Setting up a Mac mini as a home theater PC (HTPC) using its HDMI port for HD video and audio output. The author weighs the cost and capability tradeoffs of Apple's latest Mac mini lineup for media streaming.

title: Marriage Equality and the Freedom of Religion

classification.summary: Marriage equality and religious freedom intersect in ways often overlooked by traditional-marriage advocates. The author argues that codifying specific religious doctrines into constitutional law undermines freedom of religion for everyone.

A personal reflection on the GM IPO and shifting investment strategies, written as the holiday season approaches. The post blends financial commentary with a note about a changed personal circumstance during the holidays.

title: The Best Defense is No Defense

classification.summary: Martial arts philosophy, particularly hapkido and aikido, teaches that the best self-defense is avoiding conflict entirely. The post explores practical strategies for de-escalating or escaping dangerous situations rather than engaging.

title: Managing Your Identity and Cyber Security

classification.summary: Practical guidance on managing passwords and protecting personal identity online. An IT professional outlines the evolving landscape of cyber threats, from rogue hackers to state-sponsored attacks, and the personal responsibility required to stay secure.

title: Tax Reforms and Responsibility of Public Services

classification.summary: A political commentary on tax reform, public services, and fiscal responsibility at both state and federal levels. The post argues for reforming the tax code while examining the tensions between taxation history, public welfare, and government spending.

Three Iowa Supreme Court justices were removed via retention vote, raising alarms about judicial independence and the influence of religious tests on legal interpretation. The author argues this outcome prioritizes mob rule and campaign fundraising over constitutional principles.

An overview of Web 2.0 social media technologies including Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, and blogging platforms. Framed as a business communication presentation summary covering social networking channels and traffic analysis tools.

title: Social Media Basic Policies for Companies

classification.summary: A bullet-point outline of social media policies for companies, covering HR, legal, IT security, confidentiality, and public relations considerations. Practical guidance for organizations on communicating and enforcing appropriate employee conduct online.

An outline of social media strategy fundamentals including target-setting, scope, presence-building, relationship nurturing, and SWOT analysis. The post covers how organizations can evaluate and implement social media tools aligned with their business model.

An essay on the challenge of bridging divides around LGBT rights, exploring how outsiders perceive the LGBT community and why harmful stereotypes persist. The author reflects on how to reach people with varied and sometimes destructive misconceptions about LGBT struggles.

title: A Complete New Design and Refreshed Interface

classification.summary: The author announces a domain change from wpstudios.net to tigersndragons.com and a new website design using jQuery, HTML5, and CSS3. This is a site update post about hosting, branding, and web development work.

A practical guide to effective social media habits, covering privacy settings, reputation management, and mindful posting. Tips address what to share, who follows you, and how online content persists beyond any privacy controls.

title: Holiday Interests of a Different Kind

classification.summary: Holiday festivities and Eastern religious philosophies intersect in a personal reflection on the meaning beyond seasonal celebration. Drawing on Buddhism and Taoism alongside Christian tradition, the author asks what sustains compassion and happiness once the holiday season ends.

The construction of a new MLB stadium in Miami highlights a troubling irony: rising ticket prices and lavish public investment in sports entertainment coincide with foreclosures, unemployment, and cuts to critical public services. The author frames this as a revealing indicator of misaligned political priorities.

title: Form Follows Function: An Energy Strategy

classification.summary: Energy policy and the concept of 'form follows function' applied to political campaign rhetoric around mining and wind farms. The post analyzes competing energy strategies proposed by political campaigns in the 2012 election cycle.

A political essay comparing marijuana legalization debates in Colorado and Washington with recent mass shootings, framed around personal liberty versus government regulation. No lectionary or theological basis; this is public policy commentary written in December 2012.

Complacency is identified as the greatest obstacle to meaningful reform, with particular focus on ongoing inequality facing the LGBT community across the United States. The post argues that recent milestones should not breed satisfaction while federal and state-level inequities persist.

Surviving bullying shapes a resilience and pride that outsiders cannot fully understand. The piece reflects on LGBTQ experience, hypocrisy in religious condemnation, and the dignity found in having endured and risen above cruelty.

Spring renewal becomes a lens for examining resistance to change and the call to repentance. Inspired by a sermon quote about the 'old order,' the piece reflects on releasing grudges, bad habits, and clutter to embrace new possibilities.

The NSA's mass surveillance program and its use of metadata to track citizens without their knowledge raises serious constitutional questions about the Fourth Amendment and government overreach. The post examines whether government agencies should be permitted to collect and analyze this data through secret court authorizations.

Discomfort is not something to avoid but to engage with honestly — there is something vital to learn in it. Drawing on a pastoral quote about love and truth, the piece explores how relating to discomfort leads to growth, justice, and authentic community.

A political commentary on the Tea Party movement and the 2013 government shutdown, framing the group's singular perspective as bordering on insurgency. The author critiques the movement's rhetoric and short-sighted cause as disconnected from basic civic truths.

For people who live with depression, joy is a daily struggle — not an abstraction but a lived reality. The author shares personal experience with mental illness and depression to reduce stigma and offer solidarity to others who may be struggling.

Mental illness and depression affect people of all ages and backgrounds, yet social stigma shapes how we imagine it. This personal essay explores diverse ways people experience depression, drawing on the author's own journey.

Music and simple gifts offer a way to encounter God in everyday life, drawing disparate and politically divided people into common spaces. The author reflects on music's unique power to unite where other pursuits—sports, commerce—fall short.

A commentary on poverty, wages, and the welfare system in America, examining public skepticism toward anti-poverty programs. The piece engages social and political arguments about government assistance and who benefits from or abuses safety-net programs.

A personal tip on the Smith System's 5 Keys to Safe Driving, prompted by questions about the author's habit of backing into parking spaces. The post shares a link to the Smith System PDF resource with no theological or lectionary content.

Tips for ministers and church leaders navigating social media, drawn from a software engineer's professional background in technology and cloud services. The post cautions against jumping into church social media without a serious, vetted plan.

title: Changing Devotions and Perspectives: The Calling?

classification.summary: A personal essay exploring the question of divine calling amid life's wounds and shifting worldviews—martial arts, Asian philosophy, Catholic teaching, and grief. The author reflects on how finding Plymouth Congregational Church may offer clues to what God asks of them today.

title: Issues of 2016: An Abstract and Framework

classification.summary: A 14-point policy framework covering taxes, health care, guns, and other priorities ahead of the 2016 presidential election. Written from a self-described moderate perspective to guide the author's vote in the coming campaign.

A fragmented poem exploring isolation, loss of worth, and quiet crisis — images of burned bridges, empty smiles, and hidden horizons. The piece resists easy resolution, sitting with darkness and the weight of diminishment.

A personal devotional reflection written for the Deacons of Plymouth Church explores how one's experience of God and faith shifts from youth to adulthood. The author traces a journey marked by tension with institutional religion and the spiritual questions that shape identity and calling today.

Winter's many religious festivals — from Christmas to Hanukkah to Buddhist enlightenment — share a common thread of light overcoming darkness. The essay argues that beneath holiday commercialization, each tradition calls humanity toward virtue, love, and compassion over greed and destruction.

Luke 10:1-11, 16-20 sends seventy missionaries out ahead of Jesus with instructions for how to travel, who to greet, and what to proclaim. The sermon explores how we begin a mission by choosing a place and how we conduct ourselves faithfully along the way.

Luke 11:1-13 and Colossians 2:6-15 anchor this sermon on living faith and the Golden Rule. Drawing on the image of a missed task and customer disappointment, the preacher explores what it means to embody the change we seek through prayer and active discipleship.

Jeremiah, Psalm 81, Hebrews, and Luke 14 ground this sermon's central question: Does God cry? Drawing on images of divine love met with human unfaithfulness, the sermon invites reflection on God's grief and what it means to live faithfully in response.

title: Taking a Step on a Journey

classification.summary: A personal statement delivered at the beginning of a ministerial discernment process, expressing gratitude to the congregation and committee for their support. The author reflects on the privilege and responsibility of being called to represent others as a minister.

A prayer-poem wrestling with fear, division, and the call to trust in the Spirit's guidance during a time of national uncertainty. The piece invites readers to reach across cultural divides and find common ground in shared humanity.

title: Why Did You Do it - Exodus 32

classification.summary: Exodus 32:1-14 raises the haunting question of why Aaron led Israel into idolatry while Moses was on the mountain. Drawing on Moses' intercession and Jesus' cry of forgiveness from the cross, the sermon explores human failure, accountability, and divine mercy.

A poem contemplating the unsettling moment when a phone call brings unwelcome news, evoking mortality, unfinished relationships, and words left unsaid. The piece meditates on reconciling sudden alarm with the things one has yet to do or say.

Isaiah 6:1-8 opens with a breathtaking vision of the LORD enthroned in glory, attended by seraphim, as Isaiah encounters both divine holiness and forgiveness. The sermon invites the congregation to imagine themselves in that scene and to respond eagerly—like Isaiah—to God's call: 'Here am I; send me.'

A pastoral prayer offered on the fifteenth anniversary of September 11, 2001, lifting up grief, healing, and gratitude for first responders. The prayer calls the congregation to redeem tragedy through service, forgiveness, and trust in God's guidance.

title: On the nature of God and evil.

classification.summary: A reflection on the nature of God and the problem of evil. The body opening provides no further detail beyond the title, making the content and argument unclear.

A memorial tribute to Ernest Hansen, the author's father, honoring his military service and faithful life. Scripture from Isaiah 6:8 and Hebrews 13 frames his legacy of duty, love, and commendation to God.

Luke 3:1-22 and the people's expectation of the Messiah frame this Epiphany sermon on John the Baptist's proclamation. The sermon explores what it means to carry expectations—of ourselves, of others, and of God—and how baptism reorients those hopes toward Christ.

Reading the Bible invites layers of interpretation shaped by historical context, faith, and congregational perspective. Drawing on experience as a Deacon and Stephen Minister, the author explores how multiple influences on the text open new ways the Word can teach us.
A post sharing a link or promotion for the UCC Stillspeaking Daily Devotional. No original sermon, reflection, or liturgical content is present in the body.

title: On the Nature of God and evil (revised)

classification.summary: The nature of God—both loving and seemingly complicit in tragedy—poses a central paradox for Judeo-Christian faith. The author argues that God is a growing, evolving presence throughout Scripture, offering a way to hold love alongside suffering.

Luke 13:1-9, 31-35 calls us to honest reckoning with repentance and change during the Lenten season. The sermon explores why 'change' rings hollow in public life yet carries urgent, personal weight in the way of Jesus.

A personal account of a family's experience with a father's diagnosis of a rare, incurable leukemia. The author describes the shock of the news and the gradual onset of serious symptoms requiring hospitalization.

Good Friday invites Christians to remember the torture, betrayal, and state execution of Jesus alongside the Passover liberation celebrated by Jewish neighbors. The reflection traces the full arc of degradation and communal violence that led to the cross.

A reflection on the Netflix series '13 Reasons Why' exploring its portrayal of bullying, depression, and suicide among adolescents and adults. The author considers the merits of the show's approach and its capacity to prompt difficult but necessary conversations.

title: Always Room for One More - Acts 15

classification.summary: Acts 15:1-18 takes center stage as the apostles debate the terms of belonging in the early church. The sermon explores how the Jerusalem Council's deliberations speak to radical inclusion and who gets welcomed into God's community.

Luke 19:29-44 anchors this Palm Sunday sermon on Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem. The preacher explores what it means to join a worthy procession, drawing on the contrast between worldly power and the humble, donkey-riding messiah.

A five-week adult education course outline tracing Christian history from Acts through the formation of the UCC. The syllabus aims to help participants understand how persecution, reform, and tradition shaped the church across centuries.

John 10:1-10 and Psalm 23 frame Jesus as the good shepherd whose voice calls his own, while others are named 'thieves of sheep.' The sermon invites reflection on who we follow and whose voice we trust to lead us toward abundant life.

A pastoral prayer offered in corporate worship, inviting the congregation into quiet meditation and humble gratitude before God. It calls the gathered community to trust divine wisdom, seek mercy, and remember neighbors in need of compassion.

title: When No One Will Listen - Matthew 11

classification.summary: Matthew 11 confronts the human tendency to hear without truly listening, as Jesus and John the Baptist face rejection and cynicism from their communities. The sermon explores what it means to receive difficult messages and why societies resist prophetic voices.

Genesis 1:26-31 frames a devotional meditation on humanity's call to care for all creation, drawing on tai chi breathwork and embodied presence. The reflection invites participants to feel the energy of the world around them as a spiritual practice rooted in the image of God.
notes: Body references Genesis 1:26-31 but reads more like a guided devotional exercise than a lectionary-based reflection — human reviewer should confirm whether this fits the sermon/reflection series or belongs elsewhere

title: Stories, Beloved and Gardens to Tend

classification.summary: A liturgical prayer reflection on being God's Beloved, grateful for shared stories and called to tend the garden of creation. The piece weaves wonder and vocation with ecological awareness, inviting worshippers to care for the world entrusted to them.
notes: Content appears to be a liturgical prayer or worship resource rather than a full service order — reviewer should determine if it fits worship_resources or should be retired

classification.summary: The parable of the wicked tenants in Matthew 21 becomes a lens for examining the nature of privilege—as honor, status, and ultimately something to be relinquished. Drawing on Exodus 20, Psalm 19, and Philippians 3, the sermon challenges hearers to consider how clinging to privilege conflicts with faithful discipleship.

title: Letting Go of Being Right - Matthew 5: 1-12 All Saints

classification.summary: A reflection on All Saints Day draws from Revelation 7, Psalm 34, 1 John 3, and the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:1-12) to explore the theme of letting go of being right. Preached as a vespers service, it connects the communion of saints with humility and the grace that defines God's people.

title: Hope in a world of Anxiety - Mark 13

classification.summary: Isaiah 9:2, Psalm 80, and Mark 13:24-27 frame an Advent meditation on hope amid anxiety. Drawing on the feast of St. Nicholas, the sermon explores how ancient promises of light breaking into darkness speak to a world gripped by fear.

On New Year's Eve, a meditation on facing uncertain and scary times draws on Rocky IV and Ecclesiastes 3. The reflection considers how pressing through difficulty—going one more round—is what makes all the difference in life.

A theology of worship explores both intensive (corporate) and extensive (personal) ways of approaching God, drawing on liturgical traditions across Catholic, Buddhist, and Protestant contexts. The essay argues that worship integrates faith and life and is the core activity of a gathered community.
notes: Appears to be a theological essay or academic reflection rather than a sermon or liturgy; no RCL lectionary basis named in opening

classification.summary: Based on 1 Thessalonians 5:1-11, this sermon explores the metaphor of putting on a hat — how it can signal transformation, courage, and wisdom, or be used to hide from a fearful world. The preacher connects Paul's armor of light imagery to the call to remain awake and sober amid racial injustice, violence, and chaos.

Luke 6:20-42 anchors this Lenten reflection on Jesus's Sermon on the Plain, where blessings and woes reframe human values. The passage's call to love enemies and release judgment offers a demanding path of discipleship for the Lenten journey.
notes: title suggests sermon on Luke 6:20-42 during Lent 2019 but no body content to confirm

title: Living in the Way of God - Matthew 5:21-37

classification.summary: Matthew 5:21-37, paired with Deuteronomy 30 and Psalm 119, calls believers to embody God's commandments not just in outward action but in the depths of the heart. Jesus' teaching following the Beatitudes challenges comfortable faith and invites a way of living shaped by divine love.

title: I am the bread of life

classification.summary: Jesus declares 'I am the bread of life' in John 6:35, set in the context of feeding thousands and a crowd still hungry for signs. The reflection explores what it means to find sustenance and satisfaction in Christ beyond physical bread.
notes: Date is 2020-02-29 which falls on a Saturday — unclear if this was preached or written as a standalone reflection; no explicit 'Sermon based upon' framing detected

classification.summary: John 8:12 — 'I am the light of the world' — anchors this Lenten reflection on humanity's strange comfort in darkness. Drawing on the story of the woman caught in adultery, the sermon explores why we resist turning toward the light even when it is freely offered.

Jesus declares 'I am the gate' in John 10, offering both protection and passage for those who enter. The sermon explores what it means to cross thresholds of faith, drawing on the varied gates of scripture, literature, and everyday life.

Matthew 20:1-16's parable of the vineyard workers challenges human notions of fairness and what we think we deserve. Drawing on Jonah 4, Psalm 145, and Philippians 1, this sermon explores how the kingdom of God upends social norms and reveals unexpected grace.

title: Worship Bulletin for Aug 30, 2020 - St John UCC

classification.summary: A full worship bulletin for St. John UCC's Thirteenth Sunday after Pentecost, August 30, 2020. The service includes a call to praise drawn from Psalm 105, a Maya Angelou poem as an invitation, and the congregation's welcoming affirmation.

Matthew 21:23-32 raises the question of what authority governs our lives, set against Psalm 25, Exodus 17, and Philippians 2. The sermon explores how presumptions about power and authority—timely in an election season—give way to the humble, servant authority modeled by Christ.

Mark 13:24-37, Psalm 80, and Isaiah 64:1-9 anchor this Advent 1 sermon on hope amid the COVID-19 pandemic. The preacher asks why and how we remain thankful and expectant even in exhaustion and uncertainty.

Isaiah 61's proclamation of good news and Psalm 126's songs of restoration anchor this Third Sunday of Advent reflection on joy amid pandemic fatigue. The sermon explores how online worship has unexpectedly opened the church to new seekers, revealing glimmers of light in a difficult season.

Drawn from Ecclesiastes 3:1-8, Mark 1:4-11, and Psalm 51, this sermon responds to the January 6th Capitol attack with pastoral care and theological grounding. The preacher addresses communal anxiety and grief, asking where we go from here in light of scripture's call to renewal.

Matthew 6:1-6, 16-21 and Psalm 51 call us to examine where our true treasure lies. Jesus challenges us to practice piety — prayer, almsgiving, and fasting — from the heart rather than for outward recognition.

Mark 1:14-20 and Jonah 3 call hearers to repentance as Jesus announces the kingdom of God has come near. The sermon explores the urgency of that proclamation and what it means to believe and follow in response.

Mark 11:1-11 and Psalm 118 anchor this Palm Sunday sermon on what it means to walk with Jesus through Holy Week. The crowd's shift from 'hosanna' to 'crucify him' reveals the fragility of human witness and the call to hold fast to hope.

"Cancel culture" debates over Dr. Seuss and the Muppets obscure centuries of systemic erasure of entire communities. The post argues that the real cancel culture has long targeted marginalized groups, not children's media.

Mark 14–15 confronts us with the Passion of Christ and the question of who bears blame for violence and injustice. Honoring Jesus's sacrifice means raising a prophetic voice against corruption and trusting that even perverse power cannot extinguish what prevails.

Psalm 23, John 10:1-18, and 1 John 3:16-24 form the basis for this Easter reflection on Jesus as the Good Shepherd. Drawing on walks along a nearby creek, the sermon explores how divine guidance brings peace, presence, and belonging to our lives.

The parable of the mustard seed in Mark 4:26-32 opens a window into how the kingdom of God grows in ways we cannot fully explain or control. Drawing on 2 Corinthians 5 and Psalm 92, this sermon explores the quiet, persistent growth of faith as a source of hope.

First John 4:7-21 and John 15:1-8 anchor this Easter-season sermon on abiding in love. Jesus as the true vine frames what it means for the church to love one another as the living body of Christ in the world.

Acts 1:15-17, 21-26 and companions for the seventh Sunday of Easter raise questions of heritage, eternal life, and faithful discipleship. The replacement of Judas with Matthias grounds the church's identity in its roots—the twelve tribes, Jewish tradition, and the lessons we inherit.

Mark 3:20-41 asks who truly belongs to Jesus's family as scribes accuse him of demonic power and his own relatives question his sanity. The sermon explores how Jesus redefines family around those who do God's will, drawing on Lincoln's use of the 'house divided' image.

Mark 8:27-38 presents two pivotal moments: Peter's declaration of Jesus and the call to take up the cross. The sermon challenges hearers to wrestle with who they say Jesus is and what it means to follow him through self-denial and sacrifice.

David's abuse of power in 2 Samuel 11 exposes how manipulation and entitlement cause profound harm to vulnerable people. Drawing on Psalm 14 and Ephesians 3, this reflection connects the biblical critique of kings to contemporary calls for accountability and empowerment.

Mark 9:30-37 confronts the disciples' argument about who is greatest, reframing greatness as servant leadership and welcoming the least. Drawing on James 3–4 and Psalm 54, this sermon asks why we rush to be first and what it means to put others ahead of ourselves.

title: Bless You, Child of God - Mark 10

classification.summary: Mark 10:13-16, Psalm 8, and Genesis 2:18-24 ground this sermon in Jesus welcoming the children. The preacher reflects on childlike curiosity, imagination, and openness as the posture God calls all people to embody.

Luke 21's apocalyptic vision opens the Advent season with images of upheaval and cosmic sign — more Star Wars than Silent Night. Yet amid foreboding and long odds, the passage insists that hope is precisely what endures when everything familiar shakes.

Mark 13:1-8 anchors this reflection on the signs that surround us—cosmic, commercial, and personal—and what they truly reveal. Drawing also on Hebrews 10 and Hannah's song in 1 Samuel 2, the sermon asks what signs Mark's Gospel has uncovered about faithful living amid uncertainty.

Luke 3 and Isaiah 43 frame the Baptism of the Lord as a festival celebrating what baptism means for Jesus and for us. The sermon explores how baptism marks identity, calls believers to let go of worldly desires, and anchors Jesus's ministry.

Luke 6:17-26 and the Beatitudes invite reflection on what it means to be truly blessed, especially for those who struggle materially. Drawing on a personal story of a working-class upbringing, the sermon explores how God's blessings are bestowed on the overlooked and the poor.

Paul's hymn to love in 1 Corinthians 13, paired with Psalm 71 and Luke 4:21-30, grounds the familiar wedding passage in its original context: a divided church in Corinth. The sermon explores what self-giving love demands of community life beyond sentimentality and decoration.

Luke 4:21-30 recounts Jesus's rejection at Nazareth, where hometown crowds moved from wonder to murderous rage. The sermon asks why deeply-held viewpoints lead us to close ourselves off from others — and from God's expanding vision of grace.

Luke 9:28-36, the Transfiguration, frames a reflection on mountaintop moments and shifting perspective. Drawing on a cross-country road trip memory, the sermon explores how glimpses of the divine reshape how we see Jesus, ourselves, and our calling.

Psalm 10 is recast as a lament for the people of Ukraine amid invasion, applying the psalmist's cry against arrogant oppressors to a contemporary crisis. The reflection calls on God to rise up against those who prey on the innocent and claim divine indifference.
notes: post appears to be a creative paraphrase or adaptation of Psalm 10 applied to the Russia-Ukraine war — reviewer should confirm whether this was preached or written as a standalone reflection

classification.summary: Luke 24:1-12 and Psalm 118 proclaim Easter as miracle: the empty tomb moves from confusion to recognition of God's power over violence and death. The sermon declares that resurrection happens because every person is worthy of God's prevailing love.

Psalm 27 adapted for communal worship, recast from singular first-person to collective 'we' to invite shared trust amid suffering and uncertainty. The adaptation breaks the psalm into titled sections for meditative use, offering calm and confidence in the face of illness, chaos, and personal trial.

Luke 15:11-32 frames the parable of the prodigal son as a homecoming party celebrating what was lost being found. The sermon draws parallels to the Jacob-Esau rivalry and invites listeners to see themselves in both siblings as God's inclusive welcome is proclaimed.

Acts 9:36-43, Psalm 23, and John 10:22-30 converge on the 4th Sunday of Easter (Good Shepherd Sunday) to proclaim resurrection life. The story of Tabitha/Dorcas and the voice of the Good Shepherd invite the community to hear and embody hope.

Luke 10:38-42 and Psalm 15 frame a reflection on Mary and Martha as two models of hospitality and discipleship. The preacher connects the story to a personal call narrative, exploring what it means to sit with Jesus and truly listen to wisdom.

Luke 8:26-38 brings us a tormented man living among the dead, chained and excluded — and his transformative encounter with Jesus. The sermon explores mental health, shame, and how Christ's liberating presence restores wholeness and belonging to those cast aside.

Luke 17:11-19 tells the story of the ten lepers healed by Jesus, only one of whom returns to give thanks. The sermon explores how genuine gratitude calls us beyond polite acknowledgment to a deeper recognition of God's grace in our lives.

title: Prayer of Four Directions - Lakota Prayer

classification.summary: A Prayer of the Four Directions, drawing on Lakota spiritual tradition, invokes blessings from east, south, west, and north. Each direction carries a distinct petition — for guidance, nourishment, healing sleep, and renewal — addressed to the Lord.
notes: Liturgical resource drawing on Indigenous (Lakota) tradition — author may want to review for cultural appropriation concerns before publishing
Content is truncated; full prayer text may include additional directions or closing

classification.summary: Luke 14:25-33 calls disciples to count the cost of following Jesus — giving up everything to take up the cross. True discipleship moves beyond celebrations and comfortable faith into a life shaped by sacrifice and commitment.

Luke 15:1-10 offers twin parables of the lost sheep and lost coin, images of God's relentless search for what is missing. The sermon explores the deep joy of being found — and what it means to be sought by a God who never stops looking.

Luke 20:27-38 raises the Sadducees' challenge about resurrection through the lens of a woman passed between seven brothers—a striking image of patriarchal tradition using people as pawns. The sermon probes what resurrection truly means and how it transcends the limits of earthly social arrangements.

title: Why I Fly Military Flags commentary

classification.summary: A Veterans Day reflection on the author's father's military service during the Vietnam era and the complicated legacy of how veterans were treated upon return. The piece explores personal memory, pride in service, and the painful contradictions of American military culture.

title: Get Down from Your Tree - Luke 19

classification.summary: Zacchaeus climbs a tree to see Jesus in Luke 19:1-10, but Jesus calls him down and into genuine encounter. The sermon explores what it means to descend from our self-made perches of privilege and control to receive grace and transformation.

John the Baptist's call to "bear fruit worthy of repentance" (Matthew 3:1-12) anchors this Advent 2A sermon. The preacher explores what genuine repentance looks like through a vivid encounter on a trail, inviting the congregation into transformative preparation for Christ's coming.

Matthew 1:18-25 and Isaiah 7:10-16 frame the Advent 4A call to take responsibility as Joseph did — choosing faithfulness when facing uncertainty. The sermon explores how God's unexpected interventions invite us to step into vocation rather than walk away from it.

Matthew 4:12-23 opens with Jesus withdrawing after hearing of John's arrest — a strikingly human response to bad news. The sermon explores how fear and grief can move us toward action, as Jesus pivots from retreat to calling disciples and proclaiming light in darkness.

Matthew 6:1-6, 16-21 and Psalm 51 anchor this Ash Wednesday reflection on authentic spiritual practice as Lent begins. Rather than simply 'giving up something,' the sermon invites inward attention to how we allocate time, devotion, and heart.

title: Reflections for Lessons and Carols 2022 (Year A)

classification.summary: Reflections written for a Lessons and Carols service in Advent 2022, drawing on Genesis, covenant, and creation. Each reflection invites worshipers into the gifts of relationship with God and the call to stewardship of creation.

John 3:1-17 places the beloved 'God so loved the world' verse in its fuller context: a nighttime conversation between Jesus and Nicodemus, a Pharisee wrestling with preconceived notions of God. The sermon explores how curious questions open us to deeper revelation across the Lenten journey through John's Gospel.

Matthew 28:1-10 and Psalm 118 ground this Easter Sunday sermon on the magnificent miracle of the empty tomb. Discovery, confusion, and recognition at the tomb point to God's love prevailing over violence and anguish, affirming that every person is worthy of resurrection hope.

title: Service of Tenebrae (Matthew Year A) - Maundy Thursday

classification.summary: A Tenebrae service for Maundy Thursday traces the final hours of Jesus through Matthew 26–27, from betrayal and denial to crucifixion and death. Each shadow is marked by responsive prayer and the gradual extinguishing of candles, culminating in the Paschal Candle.

title: Story of Creation - Genesis 1 - Trinity A

classification.summary: Genesis 1 and Trinity Sunday invite us to see creation not as a past event but as an ongoing act of God still unfolding today. Preached on Trinity A, the sermon explores what it means to be awakened to the sacred work of a Creator who never stopped creating.

title: What Stories do we tell - Matthew 14, Genesis 32 -  Proper 13A

classification.summary: Matthew 14's feeding of the five thousand and Genesis 32's wrestling with God anchor a reflection on the stories that sustain and transform us. The sermon explores how recognizing God in shared meals and moments of struggle opens new perspective for disciples and community alike.

title: How to be a friendly stranger - Matthew 9 - Proper 6A

classification.summary: Matthew 9:35–10:8 calls disciples into the harvest as friendly strangers who bring healing and welcome to the world. Drawing on a garden metaphor, this sermon explores what it means to be sent out with compassion as both laborers and neighbors.

title: Living Love in the Moment - Matthew 18 - Proper 18A

classification.summary: Matthew 18:15-20 calls the community to navigate conflict and tension while recognizing Christ's presence in the midst of struggle. Drawing on Romans 13's command to love as the fulfilling of the law, this sermon explores how living love means seeing Jesus in difficult moments, not just joyful ones.

title: What do you Expect for Yourself but not for others - Matthew 18 Proper 19A

classification.summary: Matthew 18:21-35 confronts the gap between the mercy we expect for ourselves and the mercy we withhold from others. Drawing on Psalm 103 and Romans 14, this sermon asks why we accept grace so readily yet struggle to extend it to those around us.

title: The Great Loving Reformed Church - Matthew 22 John 8 - Proper 25A

classification.summary: Leviticus 19's command to love God and neighbor—and Jesus's teaching in Matthew 22—anchor this reflection on radical, unconditional love. The sermon explores what it means for the church to be truly reformed by love, without precondition or judgment.
Matthew 25:1-13 poses the question: what is the cost of foolishness? Readiness for the kingdom means more than words or appearances — it demands a prepared mind and active faithfulness.

title: For the Least of Us - Matthew 25 - Reign of Christ

classification.summary: Matthew 25:31-46 and Psalm 100 anchor this Reign of Christ Sunday sermon on care for the vulnerable. The preacher challenges assumptions about poverty and calls the congregation to see Christ in 'the least of these.'

title: Lessons and Carols (with Reflections) - Christmas - Year B

classification.summary: A Christmas Eve Lessons and Carols service for Year B, coinciding with the Fourth Sunday of Advent. The liturgy weaves Mary's Magnificat, opening prayer, and reflections into a full service order welcoming the Love of God amid trials and hope.

title: Mountains of Faith - Mark 9 - Transfiguration - Year B

classification.summary: Mark 9:2-9 recounts the Transfiguration, where Jesus is revealed in dazzling glory on a mountaintop before his disciples. Drawing on the awe of mountain landscapes, this sermon explores what it means to encounter the holy and descend transformed into ordinary life.

John 1:43-51, Psalm 139, and 1 Samuel 3 anchor this reflection on what it means to be called by Jesus. Unlike politicians driven by ego and ambition, Jesus's call to follow is rooted in purpose and genuine invitation.

Just after his baptism, Jesus is immediately driven into the wilderness by the Spirit — a jarring contrast to the affirmation 'You are my Beloved.' The sermon explores what it means that the same Spirit who blesses also pushes us into hard and formative places.

On the Second Sunday of Easter, Jesus appears to Thomas—not to leave him behind, but to meet him where he is. The resurrection is an open invitation to all, no matter where they are on life's journey.

1 John 5:1-6, Psalm 98, and John 15:9-17 ground a meditation on love as the core commandment of Christian life. The sermon explores how loving God and one another casts out fear and bears fruit in community.

title: Should I Forgive - Mark 5 (w 2 Samuel 1) - Proper 8B

classification.summary: Mark 5's two women — a dying daughter and a hemorrhaging outcast — reveal how Jesus crosses boundaries of privilege and purity to restore the forgotten. The sermon asks whether forgiveness and healing are available even to those society has discarded.

title: Unity, inclusion and remembrance - Ephesians 2 - Proper 11B

classification.summary: Ephesians 2:11-22 proclaims that those once estranged from God are now fellow citizens with the saints. The sermon explores unity, inclusion, and remembrance as calls to recognize our shared identity in Christ rather than tools of division.

title: Abuse of Power - 2 Samuel 11 - Proper 12 Year B

classification.summary: David's abuse of power in 2 Samuel 11 is examined as a #MeToo moment in scripture, refusing romanticization. The sermon calls for honest reckoning with assault, dominance, and coercion as dark realities woven into the biblical narrative.

title: Practice Action Faith - James 2- Proper 18 Year B

classification.summary: James 2:1-17 challenges believers to reject partiality based on social status and put faith into action through neighbor-love. The sermon explores what 'action faith' looks like when confronting favoritism and the command to love one's neighbor as oneself.

Mark 10:35-45 challenges James and John's request for seats of honor beside Jesus, revealing a kingdom where greatness is measured by service. The reflection explores what it truly means to be first in a community shaped by self-giving love rather than status or power.

title: What is Christian - John 18 - Reign of Christ (Year B)

classification.summary: On Reign of Christ Sunday, John 18:33-37 places Pilate's question—'Are you the King of the Jews?'—at the center of a deeper question about what it truly means to be Christian. The sermon warns against deception in desperate times and calls listeners to clarity about whose kingdom they serve.

title: Lessons and Carols - Christmas Eve - Year C

classification.summary: A full Christmas Eve 'Lessons and Carols' liturgy for Year C, structured around scripture readings from Genesis, Micah, and Luke interspersed with classic carols. The service moves from Creation and Covenant through the Nativity, welcoming all on life's journey in the UCC tradition.

Psalm 29 and the voice from heaven at Jesus' baptism (Luke 3:15-22) prompt reflection on what divine voice sounds and feels like. Drawing on experience as a martial arts teacher, the sermon explores how voice carries authority, confidence, and call.

Luke 4:21-30 recounts Jesus preaching in his hometown to a hostile reception, framed alongside Jeremiah's call and Paul's hymn to love. The sermon explores what it means to speak truth prophetically even when the community resists.

Luke 24:1-12 and John 20:19-31 anchor this Easter sermon on why resurrection is genuinely difficult to believe. The preacher confronts the unavoidable reality of death as the reason people struggle with Easter's central claim.

title: How Casual is Your Faith - Luke 6 - Epiphany 6C

classification.summary: Luke 6:17-26 and Psalm 1 anchor this sermon's probing question: how much do we expect in return for what we give — to the church, our families, and society? The sermon challenges the transactional assumptions behind faith, generosity, and communal belonging.

title: Transformation - Luke 8 - Second Sunday after Pentecost Year C

classification.summary: Luke 8:26-39 tells of a man called Legion, transformed from naked and living among the dead to sitting clothed and in his right mind at Jesus' feet. The sermon explores what it means when healing disrupts the community around us — and what we fear more, the demons or the cure.

Luke 12:13-21 and Psalm 49 frame a meditation on the parable of the rich fool and the spiritual danger of accumulating possessions. Drawing on personal experience of scarcity and gradual stability, the preacher asks what it means to be 'rich toward God' rather than toward oneself.

title: Power of Fire - Luke 12 - Proper 15 C

classification.summary: Jesus declares he came to bring fire and division in Luke 12:49-56, upending expectations of easy peace. This sermon traces the arc of Luke 12's teachings to ask how God's disruptive fire calls disciples to radical faithfulness and honest self-examination.

Luke 13:10-17, Psalm 103, and Isaiah 58 frame a reflection on the Sabbath healing of a bent-over woman and what it means to truly honor sacred rest. The sermon asks how modern Christians observe Sabbath with gratitude and grace toward those who serve them on their day off.

John 1:1-18 and Psalm 147 open the Second Sunday of Christmas with the mystical prologue of John's Gospel. Drawing on Barbara Brown Taylor's image of an altar without walls, the reflection traces a spiral of divine presence woven through creation and incarnation.

title: Among Thieves - Luke 23 - Reign of Christ

classification.summary: Luke 23:33-43 places Jesus crucified among thieves on the Reign of Christ Sunday, the last Sunday before Advent. The sermon explores what it means that the dying Jesus offers grace and presence to those condemned alongside him, and what kind of king this reveals.
notes: title-body-scripture-mismatch - title and scripture reference indicate Reign of Christ/Luke 23, but body opening shifts to Advent/Matthew material, suggesting possible copy-paste error or wrong body attached to this post

title: Bear Good Fruit - Matthew 3 - Advent 2 A

classification.summary: Matthew 3 and John the Baptist's call to repentance frame this Advent 2A sermon on bearing good fruit. Drawing on Isaiah 11 and Psalm 72, the preacher asks why John's urgent message still speaks to us as we prepare for God's breaking into the world.

title: Reflections for Lessons and Carols - Year A 2025

classification.summary: A Lessons and Carols liturgy for Advent 2025, offering reflections on the scriptural arc from Genesis through the Nativity. Each reflection invites the congregation into the story of Creation, covenant, and the gift of Emmanuel.

Isaiah 9:1-4, Psalm 27, and Matthew 4:12-23 frame a reflection on walking in darkness and bearing light in the face of injustice. Drawing on Jesus's response to John's arrest, the sermon calls the congregation to carry the torch of ministry rather than standing idly by.

The Beatitudes of Matthew 5:1-12, Psalm 15, and Micah 6:8 call followers of Christ to align action with faith. Doing justice, loving kindness, and walking humbly are not optional extras but the essence of what it means to be the church.

title: Salt of the Earth - Matthew 5 - Epiphany 5A

classification.summary: Jesus calls his followers salt of the earth and light of the world in Matthew 5:13-20, drawing on Isaiah 58's call to justice and Psalm 112's portrait of the righteous. The sermon explores what it means to let received blessings shine outward rather than becoming ineffective dust on good food.

Matthew 4:1-11 recounts Jesus' temptations in the wilderness, a confrontation with hunger, power, and idolatry. Drawing on personal experience with competitive weight-cutting, this reflection explores what it means to resist the lures that promise shortcuts to fulfillment.

title: When Jesus Arrives - Matthew 21 - Palm Sunday Year A

classification.summary: Matthew 21:1-11 recounts Jesus' entry into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday, welcomed by the outcast and the marginalized who organically line the road with cloaks and branches. The sermon explores what it means that Jesus arrives not with pomp and permits, but among those without privilege.

Jesus meets a Samaritan woman at Jacob's well and offers her living water — a gift that transcends social boundaries and transforms identity. The sermon explores this Lenten dialogue as a window into God's radical, boundary-crossing grace available to all.

title: Power of a Story - Genesis 1 - Trinity Sunday Year A

classification.summary: Trinity Sunday readings from Genesis 1, Matthew 28, and 2 Corinthians 13 invite reflection on the foundational Christian doctrine of the Trinity. The sermon explores how God, Christ, and Spirit accompany both worshippers and doubters as disciples are sent into the world.

John 14:1-14 and Acts 7:55-60 anchor this Easter 5A reflection on awakening amid stress, confusion, and the temptation toward apathy. Drawing on Stephen's vision of the risen Christ, the sermon explores how faith re-orients us when the ground shifts beneath our feet.

Jesus promises an Advocate in John 14:15-21, assuring disciples they will not be alone in their Eastertide journey. The sermon explores how love—for God and for one another—gives rise to justice, grace, and the indwelling Spirit of truth.

The Transfiguration of Jesus in Mark 9 illuminates the Epiphany theme of divine light shining in human darkness. Drawing on 2 Corinthians 4:3-6 and Psalm 50, the sermon explores what it means to witness the glory of God in the face of Christ.

Called by name in John 1:43-51, Psalm 139, and 1 Corinthians 6:12-20, this sermon explores what it means to respond to God's call. Drawing on the legacy of Martin Luther King Jr., the preacher connects discipleship to the ongoing struggle for justice and equality.

title: Let Peace Begin With Me - Mark 1, John 14

classification.summary: Isaiah 40:1-11, Psalm 126, John 14:27, and Mark 1:1-8 anchor this Advent sermon on the call to peace. Drawing on memories of the Cold War's end, the preacher invites the congregation to move from anxiety to hope and finally to peace.

Jesus declares 'I Am the Bread of Life' in John 6, drawing the crowd into a deeper encounter with divine identity. The sermon explores what it means to hunger for something beyond the ordinary, using the Wizard of Oz as a lens for the journey toward the living Christ.

title: Love and Joy of Mary is Ours - Luke 1

classification.summary: Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) and Isaiah 61 reveal a divine promise of liberation and reversal of fortune for the humble and oppressed. The sermon explores how Mary's joy and love are not merely hers alone but a gift extended to all God's children in the final week of Advent.

title: Parallels of Persecution and Community Growth

classification.summary: Parallels between early Christian persecution described by Gonzalez and the contemporary struggles of LGBT communities reveal a striking pattern of marginalization and resilience. The essay draws on church history to illuminate how fringe communities survive and grow despite—and because of—sustained opposition.

James 2 and Mark 7 frame a meditation on faith as inseparable from action. The mother in Mark's Gospel becomes a lens for understanding how genuine faith manifests in advocacy, works, and care for the vulnerable.

title: The “I am” statements in the Gospel of John.

classification.summary: The seven 'I am' statements in John's Gospel invite believers to discover divine presence within themselves. Each declaration — bread, gate, way, vine, shepherd, light, resurrection — becomes a prompt for inward spiritual assessment and communal life.

Romans 5:1-8 and Matthew 9:35-10:8 frame a reflection on privilege, racial justice, and the call to act in a moment of crisis. The sermon draws on Black liberation theology and the lectionary's witness to suffering and hope to ask: when is the time to respond?

A contemplative poem inspired by Dionysius and Chuang Tzu meditating on transcendence, light and darkness, and the unknowable Divine. Blending Christian apophatic theology with Taoist thought, it explores how silence and actionless-action allow the Holy to move through us.

Sunday worship program for July 26, 2020 from an Open and Affirming interfaith ministry. The service order includes a call to praise adapted from Psalm 105, meditation, and liturgical elements drawn from the day's lectionary readings.

John 3:1-17 and the familiar words of John 3:16 invite reflection on what it means to be born of the Spirit. The sermon explores the depth of God's love and the mysterious new life offered through Christ, moving beyond bumper-sticker theology to its transforming power.

title: St John United Church of Christ - Worship 27 Sep 2020

classification.summary: A complete Sunday worship order for St. John United Church of Christ (Melbourne, IA) for the Seventeenth Sunday after Pentecost, September 27, 2020. The service draws on Philippians 2:4-5 and Psalm 25, weaving scripture into calls to worship and congregational participation.

On National Coming Out Day, the author reflects on a 1995 campus rally at the University of Iowa and the struggle to resist external definitions of queer identity. The piece explores how faith communities and cultural narratives shaped—and complicated—the journey toward self-acceptance.

Matthew 22:34-46 and Leviticus 19 frame the double commandment to love God and neighbor. Drawing on Paul Tillich's insight that 'the first duty of love is to listen,' this sermon asks what genuine love requires of us in practice.

Gabriel's announcement to Mary in Luke 1 opens a reflection on what it means to be truly favored and loved. The sermon explores how divine favor meets human vulnerability, drawing Mary's Magnificat into a vision of love that uphlifts the lowly.

Matthew 25:31-46 calls the church to recognize Christ in the hungry, the stranger, and the least. Preached on Reign of Christ Sunday, this sermon explores where we see, feel, and dwell with Jesus through everyday acts of welcome and shared table.

John 1:35-51, Psalm 139, and 1 Samuel 3 frame an Epiphany reflection on the call to 'Come and See.' The sermon explores how Jesus invites followers into relationship rather than spectacle, drawing on personal stories of desire and contentment to illuminate discipleship.

Ecclesiastes 3:1-8 frames a pastoral response to the January 6, 2021 Capitol attack, reflecting on seasons of grief, anger, and healing. Drawing on the scriptural promise that there is a time for every purpose, the author calls the community toward covenant renewal and reconciliation.
notes: Post references January 6 Capitol attack directly — author may want to review before publishing; not framed as a formal sermon with RCL basis

classification.summary: Mark 1:21-28 and Deuteronomy 18:15-20 frame a reflection on recognizing true prophetic authority. The sermon explores how we distinguish genuine teaching from false prophecy, and what it means to heed the voice of God.

Mark 1:29-39 depicts Jesus healing Simon's mother-in-law and the town gathering around him, raising questions about why people come together in faith. Drawing on Isaiah 40's 'Have you not heard?' the sermon explores how notions of cleanliness and unworthiness can keep us from doing God's work.

Jesus in Mark 8:31-38 turns his disciples' attention toward the cross, explaining the grueling path ahead. This Lent 2 sermon explores what it means to witness the cross and take up one's own in faithful discipleship.

Mark 1:12-13 places Jesus in the wilderness among wild beasts and temptations — a scene the author connects to the ongoing wilderness of pandemic life. Drawing on Psalm 25 and 1 Peter 3:18-22, the sermon explores spiritual fatigue and the sustaining presence of God in hostile seasons.

Exodus 20's Ten Commandments offer a God-given framework for navigating life with other people. The sermon explores how the covenant rules reflect God's deep knowledge of humanity and call us toward faithful community.

Jesus' image of the grain of wheat falling to the earth (John 12:20-33) grounds a reflection on death, transformation, and new life. Drawing on gardening experience and Jeremiah's new covenant, the sermon explores what it means to follow Christ through loss into abundance.

John 3:14-21 and Numbers 21:4-9 frame God's love not as judgment but as healing gift, echoing Moses lifting the bronze serpent in the wilderness. The sermon explores human impatience and complaint, and how divine grace meets us even in our lowest moments.

Mark 16's abrupt ending leaves the women at an empty tomb fleeing in terror — and leaves us with a cliffhanger. This Easter sermon explores how the silence and disruption of resurrection become an unexpected promise rather than a disappointment.

Luke 24 closes with the risen Jesus appearing to his disciples, sharing fish, and commissioning them: 'You are witnesses of these things.' As heirs of that commission, we too are called to carry forward God's promise and hospitality into an uncertain future.

Drawing on 1 John 5:1-6, Psalm 98, and John 15:9-17, this sermon explores Jesus' command to love God and neighbor as a call that extends beyond Sunday worship into everyday life. God's commandments are not burdensome but rather a liberating framework for living out faith in the world.

David's battle with Goliath is more than an underdog story — it is a tale of covenant, surprising allegiance, and fearless trust. Drawing on 1 Samuel 17-18, Mark 4:35-41, and Psalm 9, this reflection explores what it means to face overwhelming odds with faith rather than fear.

title: Spirit of the Living God - John 15

classification.summary: John 15, Psalm 104, and Acts 2 ground this Pentecost-season reflection on the Spirit of God as Advocate and living presence. Drawing on Anthony of Padua, Francis of Assisi, and Augustine, the sermon explores how the mystery of the Holy Spirit invites us into felt encounter with the divine.

title: Reach Out and Welcome Someone - Mark 5

classification.summary: Mark 5:21-43 tells of a hemorrhaging woman who reaches out in faith and is healed by Jesus, while a synagogue leader's daughter is raised from the dead. The sermon explores what this woman's bold, desperate act of faith teaches us about recognition of divine power and the welcome Jesus extends to the marginalized.

John 6:24-35 frames Jesus as the Bread of Life, the true sustenance that satisfies beyond physical hunger. Drawing on memories of his grandmother's homemade bread, the preacher explores what it means to seek and receive the living bread that God provides.

Proverbs 9, Psalm 34, and Ephesians 5:15-20 frame a meditation on what wisdom truly means. Drawing on beloved TV mentor figures, the sermon explores how wisdom is found in simplicity, shared spirit, and living carefully in community.

Ephesians 6's 'armor of God' invites us to imaginatively clothe ourselves in divine protection — truth, righteousness, salvation, and the spirit. Drawing on Psalm 84 and 1 Thessalonians 5:8, this sermon explores what it means to dress in God's holy gear amid a world full of struggle.

Mark 10:17-31 asks what we must do to inherit eternal life — and what we must be willing to give up. This sermon explores the value we place on material possessions and how releasing them opens us to the kingdom of God.

James and John ask Jesus for seats of honor at his right and left hand, moments after he describes his path to violent death in Jerusalem. The sermon explores why Jesus makes such a request ridiculous and what it means to follow a Messiah whose greatness is measured in service and sacrifice.

Blind Bartimaeus cries out to Jesus at the close of Mark 10, and his persistence wins him sight and the call to follow. The sermon explores how true sight—seeing as a disciple—transforms perspective and reverses the world's hierarchies of status.

Mark 12:38-44's story of the widow's two coins invites reflection on why and how we give. The sermon explores giving as an act of trust and partnership, expanding one's resources into work that cannot be done alone.

Luke 3:1-6 opens with the powerful rulers of the known world — yet God's word comes not to them but to John in the wilderness. The sermon explores how this prophet of priestly lineage speaks peace and preparation from the margins, not the centers of power.

A personal update about setting up a combined Christmas, Fall, and Halloween village display with a model train after finding a pet-proof location during the pandemic. The post shares a hobby milestone with no theological or lectionary content.

Luke 1:39-55 and Micah 5 frame a meditation on Mary's Magnificat as more than prayerful words — a proclamation rooted in radical hope. Two women, Elizabeth and Mary, become unlikely bearers of divine promise, revealing the depth of incarnational love.

A Christmas Eve Lessons and Carols service order from St. John UCC in Melbourne, IA, for December 24, 2021. The liturgy includes opening prayer, Christ candle lighting, hymns, and scripture lessons guiding worshipers through the story of incarnation.

title: Reflections for Lessons and Carols - Dec 2021

classification.summary: A Lessons and Carols service reflection from December 2021 moving through Genesis, covenant, and the nativity. Each section invites worshippers to receive creation, relationship, and the gift of Christ as expressions of God's enduring presence.

Ash Wednesday reflection on Matthew 6 and Psalm 51 invites a fresh perspective on Lenten practice after two years of pandemic loss and amid the war in Ukraine. Jesus calls us to authentic faith focused on God rather than performance or self-display.

Isaiah 6:1-8, Psalm 138, and Luke 5:1-11 anchor this Fifth Sunday after Epiphany sermon on the experience of being lost and undone before God. The preacher explores how Isaiah's and Peter's encounters with holiness — moments of raw inadequacy — become the very threshold of calling and grace.

Psalm 54 adapted as a prayer of vindication in response to the invasion of Ukraine, calling on God to save, vindicate, and deliver a people under assault. The psalmist's ancient cry for divine help is reframed as intercession for a nation facing ruthless aggression.

title: True self with true God - Luke 13

classification.summary: Luke 13:31-35 presents Jesus naming Herod as a fox and God as a protective hen sheltering her brood. The sermon explores true identity in God's sheltering presence against earthly powers that threaten and destroy.

Luke 4:1-13 places Jesus in the wilderness facing three temptations—hunger, prideful power, and fear—immediately after his baptism. The sermon explores what these temptations reveal about trust, identity, and the spiritual practice of resisting deception.

Psalm 109's raw cry for vindication raises hard questions about vengeance, suffering, and the call to leave retribution to God. Drawing on Matthew 5 and Romans 12, this reflection holds the psalm's honest emotion alongside Jesus' teaching on non-retaliation.

Luke 13:1-9 poses a sharp question: do people who suffer calamity deserve it because of their sins? The sermon challenges the privileged impulse to judge others' suffering as divine punishment, calling instead for honest self-examination and repentance.

title: What would the stones say - Luke 19

classification.summary: Jesus enters Jerusalem on a borrowed donkey as crowds cry hosanna, contrasting the kingdom of God with Rome's pomp and power. The sermon explores what it means to shout hosanna and why the stones themselves would cry out if we fell silent.

An adapted version of Psalm 143 recast in communal voice, blending the psalmist's cry for deliverance with a posture of corporate lament and trust. The adaptation shifts singular pronouns to plural, shaping the ancient prayer for congregational use in Lent.

Acts 9:1-20 and Psalm 30 frame two conversion encounters with Jesus — Saul struck blind on the road to Damascus, and Ananias called to welcome his former persecutor. The sermon explores how transformative moments shatter familiar paths and how no one faces such turning points alone.

Acts 16:11-15 brings Paul and Silas to the riverside women of Philippi, whose openness to the gospel embodies the African concept of Ubuntu — 'I am because we are.' The sermon explores how mutual welcome and shared story-telling form the foundation of Christian community.

Acts 2:1–21 and Psalm 104 open a Pentecost reflection on the Holy Spirit as wind and breath of change. Drawing on the Scorpions' 'Wind of Change,' the sermon explores how the Spirit moves through history, prayer, and communal longing for peace.

Romans 5:1-5 grounds Trinity Sunday's reflection on how God manifests in the world through peace, suffering, and hope. The sermon argues that God's presence—Father, Son, and Spirit—is more tangible than dogma, offering trustworthy leadership in troubled times.

title: Why We need the Samaritan? - Luke 10

classification.summary: Luke 10:25–37 — the Parable of the Good Samaritan — frames the question 'Who is my neighbor?' through the six characters in Jesus's story. The sermon explores why the despised Samaritan is the one who reveals what love of neighbor truly demands.

Luke 11:1-13 presents Jesus teaching the Lord's Prayer, inviting his followers to approach God with whole heart and mind. The reflection explores how this Lukan version broadens the prayer's audience beyond Jewish communities and what it means to ask God for the Holy Spirit.

Luke 12:32-40 calls us to examine where we place our trust and treasure. Jesus challenges his followers to release anxiety over worldly accumulation and instead root their wealth in faith, spirit, and the promises of God.

A woman bent double for eighteen years is healed by Jesus on the Sabbath in Luke 13, challenging the religious establishment's rigid rules. The sermon explores how judgment and neglect keep suffering people hidden, and what it means to be truly set free.

title: How we play the game - Luke 16

classification.summary: Luke 16:1-13's parable of the shrewd manager raises hard questions about money, loyalty, and how we live our values. Drawing on Amos 8:4-7 and Psalm 113, the sermon asks whether winning dishonestly is really winning at all.

title: Karaniya Metta Sutta: The Buddha's Words on Loving-kindness

classification.summary: The Karaniya Metta Sutta, translated from the Pali by the Amaravati Sangha, offers the Buddha's teaching on loving-kindness toward all beings. This is a presentation of a Buddhist text without Christian lectionary framing or sermon context.

Luke 18:1-8 and Genesis 32:22-31 frame a meditation on persistence in prayer, with the widow's demand for justice and Jacob's wrestling as twin images. The sermon challenges the notion of God as aloof or indifferent, inviting deeper engagement with what it means to wrestle and not lose heart.

The parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector in Luke 18:9-14 sets two contrasting postures of prayer before us. True humility before God — not self-congratulation — is the posture that opens us to grace and transformation.

Luke 21:5-19 and Isaiah 65:17-25 frame a reflection on calamity, readiness, and endurance in uncertain times. Drawing on apocalyptic imagery, the sermon calls listeners not to fear disaster but to remain grounded in faithful preparation.

On Christ the King Sunday, Jesus hangs on the cross yet promises a fellow condemned man, 'You will be with me in paradise.' This reflection explores what salvation and the reign of Christ truly mean in a broken world.
Matthew 24:36-44 opens Advent with Jesus' call to readiness for an unexpected hour. The sermon explores how watchfulness and preparation—not dread—are the true posture of Advent hope.

A Christmas Eve 'Lessons and Carols' service order for Year A (Matthew cycle), dated December 24, 2022. The full liturgy moves from Creation in Genesis through the nativity, weaving Scripture readings with hymns and prayers.

title: What Have You Seen? - Matthew 11 - Advent 3A

classification.summary: Matthew 11:2-11 places John the Baptist in prison, full of doubts — and Jesus answers not with argument but with evidence: the blind see, the lame walk. Drawing on Isaiah 35 and personal experience of job loss, the sermon explores how Advent hope meets our deepest questions with signs rather than certainty.

title: What is your path - Matthew 2 - Epiphany A

classification.summary: Matthew 2:1-12 and Isaiah 60:1-6 illuminate the season of Epiphany — the divine revelation of Jesus to the world. The sermon asks why those in power fear good news, and invites reflection on how God shows up unexpectedly to reveal life and light.

Jesus's Beatitudes in Matthew 5:1-12, paired with Micah 6:8, reveal a vision of blessing that turns conventional assumptions upside down. The sermon explores how these texts together form both a question and a declaration about what it truly means to live a blessed life.

Matthew 5:13-20 and Isaiah 58 call the community to be salt and light in the world. A too-literal lesson about over-salted eggs opens into reflection on what it means to flavor and illuminate the world with justice and faithful action.

Matthew 5:21-38 and Psalm 119 ground this Sermon on the Mount exploration of law and promises. Jesus moves beyond mere rule-following to call his followers toward a deeper integrity rooted in love and honest commitment.

Matthew 4:1-11 opens Lent with Jesus facing temptation in the wilderness, alongside Genesis 3's account of humanity's original turning away from God. The sermon explores what temptation really is — not just giving up sugar for Lent, but the deeper question of where we place our loyalty and from whom we seek guidance.

title: At the Watering Hole - John 4 - Lent 3A

classification.summary: At the watering hole in John 4, Jesus meets the Samaritan woman across every boundary that might separate them. The sermon explores how shared, ordinary spaces become sites of radical encounter, dignity, and grace.

title: Light of the World - John 6 - Lent 4A

classification.summary: John 9's story of a man born blind — healed by Jesus with mud and spit — illuminates what it means to truly see. The sermon explores how we overlook what is right before us and how the Light of the World opens eyes both physical and spiritual.

title: Who do you want Jesus to be - Matthew 21 - Lent 6A

classification.summary: Palm Sunday's triumphal entry (Matthew 21:1-11, Psalm 118) raises a probing question: who do you want Jesus to be? The sermon invites the congregation to examine their own expectations of Jesus and why those particular images of him hold such power.

Luke 24:13-35 places two disciples on the road to Emmaus, walking with a stranger they do not yet recognize as the risen Christ. The sermon explores how belief and recognition unfold slowly, through conversation, presence, and the breaking of bread.

title: I Am the Gate - John 10 Easter 4A

classification.summary: Jesus as the gate in John 10 invites reflection on the threshold between where we are and where we are called to go. On Good Shepherd Sunday, the sermon explores how Christ holds open the gate to abundant life, guiding us through whatever burdens we carry.

John 14's 'I am the way, the truth and the life' invites deeper reflection on what it truly means to follow Christ. Drawing on Thomas's honest question and the Exodus echo of 'I am,' this Easter 5A sermon explores how Jesus's seven 'I am' sayings point toward a living, embodied path.

title: Baptized into the Spirit - 1 Corinthians 12 - Pentecost A

classification.summary: On Pentecost Sunday, Paul's vision of the Spirit-baptized body in 1 Corinthians 12 is set alongside Jesus's gift of peace in John 20 and the rush of wind at Acts 2. The sermon explores how the Spirit binds believers together across difference, countering the world's pressure toward division and hate.

title: What Do You Believe - John 14 - Easter 6A

classification.summary: John 14:15-21 frames Jesus's farewell promise of the Advocate as an invitation to examine what we truly believe and hold sacred. The sermon explores how our core convictions shape how we celebrate, punish, and shame—and calls listeners to deeper self-examination.

title: When I cry to the Lord- Genesis 21 - Proper7A

classification.summary: Hagar and Ishmael are cast into the wilderness by Sarah's cruelty and Abraham's indifference, yet God hears Hagar's cry. The sermon explores how divine compassion breaks through human injustice to sustain the abandoned and forgotten.

title: Do You Hear What I Hear - Matthew 13 - Proper 10A

classification.summary: The Parable of the Sower in Matthew 13 invites reflection on how we hear and receive the word of God. Drawing on Genesis 25 and Psalm 65, the sermon explores what it means to be truly hungry—spiritually and otherwise—and how we respond to what we are given.

title: What Do You Value - Matthew 13 - Proper 12

classification.summary: Jesus's parables in Matthew 13 ask what we truly value and why — from mustard seeds to hidden treasure. Drawing on Jacob's pursuit of Rachel and the kingdom metaphors, the sermon explores how recognizing deep value shapes the choices we make.

title: Why Take Up a Cross - Matthew 16 - Proper 17A

classification.summary: Jesus calls his followers to take up their cross in Matthew 16:21-28 — an instrument of state execution transformed into a symbol of radical sacrifice. The sermon explores what this demanding call means for discipleship and why Jesus frames commitment to him in such stark, costly terms.

title: What Happens When We Reject God - Matthew 21 - Proper 22A

classification.summary: Matthew 21:33-46, Psalm 19, and Isaiah 5:1-7 form the basis of this Proper 22A sermon on what happens when humanity rejects God. The preacher explores how ego and self-determined truth lead us away from the vineyard God entrusts to us.

title: What Calf Do you Worship - Matthew 22 Exodus 32 - Proper23A

classification.summary: Exodus 32 and Matthew 22 challenge us to examine what golden calves we construct in our own lives. Drawing on Israel's idol worship and the parable of the wedding banquet, this sermon asks what we truly worship and whether we are prepared to respond to God's invitation.

title: A Voice Cries Out - Mark 1: 1-18 - Advent 2B

classification.summary: Mark 1, Psalm 85, and Isaiah 40 call out across Advent 2B with a voice that demands waking up and preparing the way. John's baptism of repentance and forgiveness offers a path toward the coming kingdom for all who have walked in darkness.

title: Lessons and Carols - Christmas - Year B

classification.summary: A full Lessons and Carols service for Christmas Year B, tracing the arc of salvation from Creation in Genesis through the Nativity. The liturgy weaves scripture readings, reflections, and classic Advent and Christmas hymns into a complete seasonal worship order.

A reflection on January 6, 2021, comparing it to December 7, 1941 as a 'day of infamy.' The piece recounts the Capitol insurrection and its disruption of the congressional certification of the 2020 election.

Genesis 1:1-5, Mark 1:4-11, and Psalm 29 anchor this Epiphany 1B sermon on beginnings — creation, baptism, and the call to a new identity. Drawing on a personal story of martial arts training, the preacher explores how God's voice over the waters marks every beginning as sacred commissioning.

title: Who Am I - Matthew 16 - Proper 16 A

classification.summary: Jesus asks his disciples 'Who do you say I am?' in Matthew 16, probing both divine identity and the nature of human perception. The sermon explores how rumors, legends, and misperceptions shape belonging and purpose—and what it means to answer truthfully.

title: Believe in the Good News - Mark 1 - Epiphany 3B

classification.summary: Jesus proclaims in Mark 1:14-20, 'The time is fulfilled, the kingdom of God has come near, repent, and believe in the good news.' The sermon unpacks four distinct parts of this call, exploring what it means that God is near and how that nearness opens new possibilities.

title: Taken By the Hand and Lifted - Mark 1 - Epiphany 5B

classification.summary: Jesus 'took her by the hand and lifted her up' — Mark 1:29-39 becomes the lens for exploring how healing touch restores strength and dignity. Drawing on a personal hospital visit, the sermon reflects on how divine presence meets us in vulnerability and raises us up.

Mark 8:31-38 confronts Peter's rebuke by Jesus at the moment he confesses Christ yet rejects the path of suffering. The sermon explores what it costs to follow Jesus when the way of the cross upends our expectations of what faithfulness looks like.

John 3:14-21 offers far more than a bumper-sticker verse: the full conversation between Jesus and Nicodemus reveals a spirit-filled tension encompassing the cross, resurrection, and ascension. The sermon invites a deeper reading of God's love and light breaking into the world during Lent.

title: For All my Sin - Psalm 51 - Lent 5B

classification.summary: Psalm 51's cry for mercy frames a Lenten meditation on repentance, forgiveness, and the correcting of injustice. Drawing on David's confession and Jeremiah's new covenant, the reflection asks who deserves our deepest apologies and how we repair the harm we have caused.

title: Maundy Thursday Service and Tenebrae - Year B

classification.summary: A complete Maundy Thursday and Tenebrae service order for Year B, drawing on Exodus 12:14, Psalm 116, and Mark 14. The liturgy moves through call to worship, confession, gathering hymns, and the darkening of lights in the Tenebrae tradition.

title: Honor the Sacrifice - Maundy Thursday  - Year B

classification.summary: Mark 14–15 grounds this Maundy Thursday reflection on the Passion of Christ, from the Last Supper to the cross. Honoring Jesus's sacrifice means raising a voice against violence and injustice, especially when it is inconvenient.

title: Walk the Way of Jesus - 1 John 3 - Easter 4B

classification.summary: First John 3:16-24, Psalm 23, John 10:11-18, and Acts 4:5-12 ground this Good Shepherd Sunday sermon on walking the way of Jesus. Knowing Jesus means more than professing love — it means actively living as Jesus lived, in concrete acts of care and discipleship.

A Pentecost blessing and prayer drawn from John 15–16, Psalm 104, and Acts 2, written for worship on the Day of Pentecost, Year B. The piece invokes the Spirit's fire and breath as a call to hear diverse voices and meet them with grace.

title: Whom Shall I Send - Isaiah 6 - John 3 - Trinity B

classification.summary: Trinity Sunday brings Jesus and Nicodemus into conversation about rebirth and the Spirit that moves like wind — mysterious and beyond our control. The sermon calls us to witness the sacred mystery that binds us as children of God, seeing past the divisions and deceptions that fracture community.

title: Bread That Fulfills - John 6 - Proper 13 B

classification.summary: John 6:24-35 and the feeding narratives invite reflection on bread that truly satisfies — not the bread of convenience or consumption, but the living bread Jesus offers. Drawing on 2 Samuel 11-12 and Psalm 51, the sermon explores hunger, forgiveness, and the grace that restores.

title: Whole Armor of God - Ephesians 6 John 6 - Proper16 B

classification.summary: Ephesians 6's 'whole armor of God' and John 6's bread of life discourse converge in a reflection on Christ's sacrifice and the Eucharist. The sermon explores why difficult teachings about flesh and blood point to the cross and the grace offered to all at the communion table.

title: What Does the Kingdom Look Like - Mark 4 - Proper 6B

classification.summary: Jesus asks, 'With what can we compare the kingdom of God?' — and answers with the parable of the mustard seed. Drawing on personal gardening experience and Ezekiel's vision of a sprig planted on a mountain, this reflection explores God's patient, abundant work growing from the smallest beginnings.

title: Grief of Divorce - Mark 10 - Proper 22 Year B

classification.summary: Mark 10:2-16 and Genesis 2:18-24 frame a deeply personal reflection on marriage and divorce. Drawing on his own experience of separation, the preacher explores what it means when 'one flesh' breaks apart and how the gospel speaks into that grief.
