Mark 12:28-34 and the Shema frame a reflection on the greatest commandment — love of God and neighbor — set against All Saints Day. Drawing on Deuteronomy 6, Psalm 119, and 1 Corinthians 13, the sermon explores love as a universal, unbranded, deeply human and sacred reality.

Luke 21:25-36, Psalm 25, and Jeremiah 33:14-16 anchor this Advent 1 reflection on the candle of hope. Amid ominous signs of distress and uncertainty, the call is to stand up and raise your heads, trusting that redemption is drawing near.

title: Preparing Our Hearts - Luke 3 - Advent 3 Year C

classification.summary: Luke 3:7-18 brings John the Baptist's urgent call to share coats, food, and honest dealings — the practical shape of repentance. The sermon presses listeners beyond hearing the good news to actually living it in everyday acts of justice and generosity.

Luke 2:41-52 brings us the only childhood story of Jesus in the Gospels — the twelve-year-old found teaching in the Temple. Drawing on his own twelfth year, the preacher explores how formative experiences shape the people we become and the callings we grow into.

John 1:1-18 and Psalm 147 anchor this Christmas 2C reflection on the Word made flesh as light shining in darkness. Drawing on the image of diverse flowers planted by God, the sermon explores identity, purpose, and the season's call to embrace who we are.

A personal reflection by Tony E. Hansen on self-examination, past choices, and the hope of new beginnings. The piece explores boundaries, regret, and gratitude without a lectionary or scriptural basis.

Luke 6:27-38 challenges us to love not just those who love us back, but even those who harm, deceive, or drain us. The sermon wrestles with how Jesus' command to love enemies intersects with modern wisdom about setting boundaries with toxic people.

title: Living Between the Already and the Not Yet - Luke 4 - Lent 1C

classification.summary: Luke 4:1-13 recounts Jesus's 40-day testing in the wilderness, where he resists empty sustenance, false power, and the temptation to test God. The sermon explores how Christians navigate the tension between what God has already accomplished and what is not yet fully realized, drawing on Psalm 91 and Deuteronomy 26.

title: What would you do - Luke 15 - Lent 4C

classification.summary: Luke 15's parable of the prodigal son invites readers to consider not just who is lost, but who needs to be found — including a fourth, often-overlooked perspective. The sermon explores the different viewpoints within the story and what it means to be welcomed home.

title: Repent because you need to - Luke 13 - Lent 3C

classification.summary: Luke 13:1-9 reframes repentance not as punishment but as a life-giving practice. Drawing on the fig tree parable and Jesus's response to tragedy, this sermon challenges the assumption that suffering is divine judgment and calls hearers toward genuine transformation.

title: Showing Love or Complicit in Betrayal - John 12 - Lent 5C

classification.summary: John 12:1-8 brings together Mary's extravagant anointing of Jesus and Judas's sharp objection, raising the question of what genuine love and faithful discipleship look like. The sermon explores whether Judas deserves his reputation or whether others' jealousy and self-interest have shaped the story we inherited.

Psalm 23, Revelation 7:9-17, and John 10:22-30 invite us to seek the Good Shepherd not only in sacred buildings but in the ordinary beauty and struggle of daily life. The reflection asks where God is found, and answers: everywhere we are connected to one another and to creation.
In the wake of Jacob's death, questions arise that have no easy answers. Faith and hope remain available even in the most desperate hours, offering possibility and strength when we most need them.

title: Time for Caring - Luke 10 - 5th Sunday after Pentecost Year C

classification.summary: Luke 10:25-37, the Parable of the Good Samaritan, anchors this sermon on what it means to truly care for our neighbors. Drawing on a personal cycling mishap, the preacher explores how we pass by those in need and what it costs us to stop and help.

title: What Would Make You Change - Luke 16 - Proper 21C

classification.summary: Luke 16:19-31, the parable of Lazarus and the rich man, frames a story of radical reversal — comfort in life becomes torment in death, and neglect of the poor carries eternal consequence. The sermon asks what it truly takes to change hearts that ignore suffering at their own gate.

title: Do Not be Afraid - Luke 12 - Proper 14 Year C

classification.summary: Luke 12:32-40 invites the community not to be afraid, anchored by God's faithfulness shown in Genesis 15 and Hebrews 11. The sermon explores how fear and misplaced treasure pull our hearts away from God, and what it looks like to trust in the kingdom instead.

title: Cost of Discipleship - Luke 17 - Proper 22 C

classification.summary: Luke 17:5-10 raises the apostles' plea — 'Increase our faith' — as the cost of radical forgiveness becomes clear. The sermon explores why genuine discipleship demands faith that moves beyond hurt and expectation of reward.

Luke 19:1-10 tells the story of Zacchaeus, the tax collector who climbs a sycamore tree to glimpse Jesus and finds himself welcomed rather than condemned. The sermon explores how Jesus challenges the crowd's snap judgments, offering grace to the least-expected recipient.

Matthew 24's call to watchfulness opens Advent 1A with an invitation to holy alertness rather than anxious vigilance. The sermon explores what it truly means to 'stay awake' as the Son of Man comes at an unexpected hour.

title: What Costume Would You Wear? - Luke 18 - Proper 25C

classification.summary: Luke 18:9-14 presents the parable of the Pharisee and tax collector, a story about the costumes of self-righteousness we wear. The sermon explores what true humility looks like versus performed piety, set against the backdrop of Halloween.

A Christmas Eve Lessons and Carols liturgy for 2025 (Year A) opening with creation in Genesis and proceeding through the traditional sequence of readings and carols. The service includes the Lord's Prayer, lighting of the Christ candle, 'Joy to the World,' and a welcome to all at the Lord's table.

Commentary on the January 6 Capitol attack anniversary and U.S. foreign policy actions toward Venezuela and Greenland. The author reflects on the trauma of the insurrection and criticizes efforts to distract from or erase its memory.

Psalm 80, Isaiah 7:10-16, and Matthew 1:18-25 frame Ahaz's refusal to ask a sign from God alongside Jesus's resistance to temptation in the wilderness. The reflection probes why we resist divine gifts and whether our desires serve God, neighbor, or ego.

title: Wisdom in Dark Days - Matthew 5 - Epiphany 6A

classification.summary: Deuteronomy 30:15-20, Matthew 5:21-37, and Psalm 119 set the stage for a meditation on choosing life and following the law in difficult times. The preacher reflects honestly on personal brokenness and paradox as a lens for understanding what faithful obedience looks like in dark days.

title: Addendum to Epiphany 6A - a reflection and commentary

classification.summary: A personal addendum to an Epiphany 6A sermon, reflecting on competing views of sexuality and sexual ethics. The author acknowledges the limits of their own perspective while also identifying gaps in traditional approaches.
notes: addendum to Epiphany 6A sermon but lacks explicit scripture or lectionary framing in this post itself — reviewer should decide whether to link or merge with the primary sermon

title: Do You Hear God - Acts 10 - Baptism of the Lord - Year A

classification.summary: At the Baptism of the Lord, Jesus submits to John's baptism of repentance, embracing a moment of conversion and calling. Drawing on Acts 10, Matthew 3, Isaiah 42, and Psalm 29, this reflection explores how God's voice breaks through water and Spirit to claim and send us.

title: What Do We Know - John 3 - Lent 2A

classification.summary: John 3:1-21 frames Nicodemus's nighttime encounter with Jesus as a meditation on what we can truly know. The sermon explores how certainty gives way to mystery when we encounter the divine, drawing on Psalm 121 and Genesis 12.

A personal essay tracing the author's spiritual journey from Catholic upbringing through Asian philosophy to UCC ministry. The reflection explores how recent writing signals a shift in the thinking and tradition that governs the author's faith.

John 9's healing of the blind man becomes a lens for examining what it truly means to see — spiritually, theologically, and communally. Drawing on Psalm 23 and 1 Samuel 16, the sermon explores how Jesus refuses self-serving displays of power, acting instead for the sake of the vulnerable.

John 11 and the valley of dry bones in Ezekiel 37 frame a reflection on what lies beyond devastation and death. Drawing on the cultural memory of Cold War nuclear fear, the sermon asks what resurrection hope means when the worst has already happened.

title: What Do You Believe - John 20 - Easter 2A

classification.summary: Psalm 16, John 20:19-31, and 1 Peter 1:3-9 anchor this Easter 2A sermon on doubting Thomas and the disciples hiding behind locked doors. The preacher draws a direct line between Thomas's demand for proof and the anxiety and uncertainty his congregation carries into worship.

Luke 24:13-35, the Emmaus road story, anchors this Easter 3A sermon on recognizing the risen Jesus in unexpected encounters. The preacher explores how we treat strangers and whether we would recognize Christ walking incognito among us.

title: Time for a Third (and more) Party

classification.summary: A critique of the American two-party system argues that both Democrats and Republicans serve corporate elites rather than the middle class. The post calls for viable third-party alternatives to break the cycle of partisan posturing.

An open letter from a gay parent to his teenage son, reflecting on life's challenges and the complexities of having a gay parent. Inspired by Langston Hughes' 'Mother to Son,' it offers personal wisdom about navigating social stigma and family identity.
A political argument in support of the Occupy movement and economic reform. The author critiques capitalism as exploiting inequality and calls for structural change beyond protest.

A political commentary on GOP obstruction and President Obama's leadership during the Great Recession. The piece argues that Republican priorities have been focused on defeating Obama rather than addressing jobs, deficits, or middle-class concerns.

A commentary on religious anger and the violence sparked by an anti-Islam video amid Middle East riots in 2012. The piece argues that religion's deeply personal nature makes offense almost inevitable, and extremism on all sides fuels conflict.

title: How to Corrupt the Government Insurance Programs

classification.summary: An analysis of Paul Ryan's 2012 proposals to privatize Social Security and Medicare, arguing that handing these programs to the private sector carries serious consequences. The piece frames the GOP vice-presidential pick as a clear ideological divergence from New Deal-era policy traditions.

Technology has transformed how we communicate, but may be replacing genuine human connection with instant, shallow interaction. Drawing on Sherry Turkle's TED talk, the piece explores how devices erode self-reflection and compassion.

A reflection on Thomas Friedman's 'flat world' globalization thesis, written for AccessLine Iowa. The piece examines claims about growing cultural interdependence against evidence that cross-cultural interactions remain limited.

Thomas Friedman's 'flat world' thesis on globalization is examined against critics who argue that cross-cultural interactions remain surprisingly limited. The piece explores the tension between idealized interdependence and the reality of national and cultural boundaries.

A deacon's remark that 'people can learn a lot from animals' sparks a personal reflection on life lessons offered by a cat named Mr. Snuggles. The essay draws gentle wisdom from pet behavior without any scriptural or lectionary grounding.

Matthew 14:22-33 and the story of Peter walking on water anchor this sermon on doubt and trust in Jesus. Drawing on Genesis 37 and Romans 10, the preacher explores how fear and uncertainty test faith — and how Christ reaches out even when we sink.

Romans 12:9-21 calls believers to let love be genuine — authentic and real. Drawing on Jeremiah, the Psalms, and Matthew 16, this sermon explores what it means to practice love that is tender, honest, and transformative in community.

Exodus 14:19-31 recounts Israel's crossing of the Red Sea — a story far richer than any Hollywood epic. The sermon moves beyond Charlton Heston and Christian Bale to recover the theological depth of divine liberation and the mystery of God's action in history.

title: Walking with Love and Patience - John 3

classification.summary: Ephesians 2:1-10, Psalm 107, and John 3:14-21 guide this Lenten vespers reflection on walking through wilderness with love and patience. Drawing on a youth lock-in discussion, the sermon explores how grace and faith sustain us through suffering and spiritual growth.

title: Walking in a Wilderness with God - Mark 1

classification.summary: Isaiah 9:2, Genesis 17:1-2, Psalm 22:23-24, and Mark 1:9-15 ground this Lenten sermon on wilderness as a place of transformation and encounter with God. Drawing on Jesus, Moses, and desert monastics, the preacher explores how darkness and trials can deepen our union with the divine.

Psalm 51, Hebrews 5:5-10, and John 12:20-33 anchor this Lenten vespers reflection on walking with prayerful humility. The preacher draws on four weeks of wilderness wandering to explore how suffering, perspective, and prayer shape the journey toward Holy Week.

title: Receive the Spirit of Peace - John 20

classification.summary: Psalm 133, John 20:19-31, and 1 John 1–2 form the basis for this Easter Season sermon on receiving the Spirit of peace. The sermon explores the Easter season's ongoing call to faith, addressing doubt and the gift of peace Christ offers his disciples.

Psalm 118 and John 12:12-16 anchor this Palm Sunday sermon on Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem. The preacher explores the courage required to follow Jesus into holy week and what it means to go forth in faith.

title: Where Do You Belong? - John 10, Psalm 23

classification.summary: Psalm 23, John 10:11-18, and 1 John 3:16-24 invite us to consider where we truly belong. The sermon explores Christ as the Good Shepherd whose call to service draws us into community, challenging us with the discomfort that faithful discipleship requires.

John 15:1-8, Psalm 22, and 1 John 4:7-21 frame a reflection on bearing fruit through love. The sermon challenges fear and divisiveness, calling the congregation to witness Christ with whole heart and mind.

Acts 1:15-17, 21-26 recounts the choosing of Matthias to restore the circle of twelve apostles, raising questions about roots, foundation, and belonging. The sermon explores what this story means for our own narratives of faith, eternal life, and how we understand our place in God's community.

A personal essay submitted for seminary (CTS) application, reflecting on the author's renewed sense of call to ministry after a period of doubt and distance from organized religion. The essay traces the author's journey from youthful calling through exile from church to return and reengagement with the UCC.

Drawn from 1 Samuel 8, Psalm 138, 2 Corinthians 4:13–5:1, and Mark 3:20-35, this reflection explores what happens when people fail — from Israel's demand for a king to the scribes' accusations against Jesus. The sermon traces how failure and forgetting shape communities, and what grace looks like in response.

Mark 6:1-13 recounts Jesus's rejection in his hometown, a familiar story of homecoming met with stubborn disbelief. The sermon explores how acquired wisdom and calling are often dismissed by those who think they already know us.

A reflection on 2 Samuel 11:1-15 connects David's abuse of power and Bathsheba's story to the contemporary #MeToo movement. The sermon argues that scripture refuses to sanitize stories of exploitation, calling the church to name and confront abuse rather than look away.

Luke 3:7-18 brings John the Baptist's sharp call to repentance and the crowd's urgent question: 'What should we do?' John's answer — share what you have, act fairly, be content — frames Advent joy as concrete, embodied action rather than mere feeling.

Mark 12:38-44 and 1 Kings 17:8-16 invite reflection on faithful generosity versus performative religion. Jesus condemns scribes who demand ritual privilege while praising the widow whose offering demonstrates genuine, sacrificial faith.

Mark 10:17-31, alongside Job 23 and Psalm 90, confronts the story of the rich man and what it means to truly detach from wealth. The sermon challenges prosperity theology and invites an interfaith exploration of radical generosity and freedom.

A personal Christmas reflection written on December 25, 2018, drawing on Psalm 19 and John 1:1 to meditate on the year past and the meaning of the season. The author writes with humility and grace, sharing personal insight rather than preaching doctrine.

title: What Do Our Dreams Tell Us - Matthew 2

classification.summary: The Magi's visit in Matthew 2 and Isaiah 60 illuminate what it means to follow divine dreams toward the light of Epiphany. The reflection explores the gifts brought to Jesus and what those gifts—and our own dreams—reveal about God's call to us.

Luke 3's account of Jesus' baptism opens a reflection on what baptism means for faith identity and communal belonging. The sermon explores how baptism calls us into relationship with God and one another, drawing on Psalm 19 and Paul's vision in 2 Corinthians.

Luke 6:27-38 and Psalm 37 form the basis of this sermon on how to live according to Jesus' teachings. Using everyday examples like traffic laws, the sermon explores whether the commands to love enemies and give generously are mere suggestions or serious callings.

Luke 5:1-11 frames a reflection on what it means to be truly seen and called, drawing on the stories of early Christian martyrs Perpetua and Justin. The author explores what ancient perspectives on martyrdom teach contemporary disciples about vocation and faithful witness.

title: The Creed and Tao of God

classification.summary: Comparing the Nicene Creed to the Tao Te Ching, this reflection explores how ancient texts shape belief and meditative practice. The author seeks language for faith that moves beyond traditional creedal formulations toward a broader spiritual vision.

title: Woe to contentment (Lenten Journeys Luke13 and Luke6)

classification.summary: Luke 13:31-35 and Psalm 27 anchor this Lenten reflection on the danger of spiritual complacency. The sermon calls congregants out of contentment and into the ongoing, unfinished work of faithful discipleship.

title: Spirit of the Living God - Pentecost Jun 9 2019

classification.summary: Acts 2:1-21 and Romans 8:14-17 anchor this Pentecost sermon on the Spirit of the Living God. Using Iowa vs. Iowa State football rivalry as a relatable opening, the preacher explores how the Spirit forms and unites a diverse community of faith.

St. Francis of Assisi's embrace of poverty and humility offers a model for Christian stewardship of creation. The reflection draws parallels between Francis, Hildegard of Bingen, and the current Pope's example of simplicity.

title: Re-visions and Re-formations: Nature of Humanity from Luther and Calvin

classification.summary: A seminary paper exploring the nature of humanity as understood by Luther and Calvin, set against Catholic notions of free will and justification. The author reflects on how these Reformation thinkers are shaping his personal theology as he serves UCC congregations with Reformed roots.

Paul's letter to the Colossians opens with a prayer for fruitfulness, paired with Deuteronomy's call to obedience and the parable of the Good Samaritan. The sermon explores what it means to bear fruit in daily life, trusting that God's presence empowers us through fear and failure.

A prophetic call to UCC churches to offer religious asylum to immigrant families facing ICE threats, framed as an act of radical Christian witness. Drawing on Paul's "now is the acceptable time," the author challenges congregations to mobilize and extend extravagant welcome.

Colossians 1:15-28 unveils the mystery hidden for ages — Christ in you, the hope of glory. Drawn alongside Genesis 18 and Luke 10:38-42, this sermon explores what it means to receive and embody that revealed mystery in daily life.

title: Where is Your Treasure - Hebrews 11:1-3,8-16, Luke 12:32-34

classification.summary: Hebrews 11 and Luke 12:32-40 ground a sermon on where we place our treasure, prompted by a mission trip to the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe reservation. The preacher connects the faith of Abraham and Sarah with a call to pursue justice and heavenly treasure over earthly security.

Hebrews 11:29–12:2 presents a sweeping chronicle of faith journeys from the Hebrew Bible, asking what this history is worth to us today. Alongside Luke 12:49-56 and Jeremiah 23:23-29, the sermon explores how the 'cloud of witnesses' calls the community to persevering, active faith.

title: What is a Sabbath  - Luke 13:10-17, Hebrews 12:18-29

classification.summary: Luke 13:10-17 and Hebrews 12:28 frame a reflection on the Sabbath as both divine commandment and gift. The sermon explores multiple perspectives—including the healing of the bent woman—to ask what Sabbath truly means for faithful living today.

Jeremiah's potter, Luke's cost of discipleship, and Psalm 139 frame a Rally Sunday meditation on building paths of faithful living. The sermon explores what it means to follow Jesus through conflict, challenged traditions, and intentional preparation for a new season.

Genesis 32:22-31 recounts Jacob's mysterious, all-night wrestling match with God — a story of struggle, surrender, and transformation. The sermon explores what it means to wrestle honestly with God, drawing on the broader lectionary texts and the author's own experience as a wrestler.

title: Justified by Faith - Romans 5 - Trinity Sunday

classification.summary: Romans 5:1-5 grounds a Trinity Sunday sermon on the deep theological doctrine of justification by faith. The preacher explores what it means to be made right with God through faith, drawing also on Proverbs 8 and Psalm 8.

A complete Lessons and Carols liturgy for Advent 2019, including opening prayer, Lord's Prayer, assurance, and reflections. The service weaves confession, praise, and anticipation through the season of waiting and preparation.

Matthew 3:13-17 brings the Baptism of Jesus and its invitation to experience the Spirit's revelation here and now. The sermon explores how baptism is a public, communal act of witness and affirmation of identity as God's beloved.

A pointed critique of the Trump administration's first term, cataloguing perceived failures across tax policy, climate change, healthcare, immigration, racial justice, and foreign policy. Written in January 2020, this post is direct political commentary without theological or lectionary framing.

Romans 12:9-18 calls believers to genuine love amid social unrest, racial injustice, and division. Drawing on Matthew 5 and the Exodus, this meditation explores what authentic, unhypocritical faith looks like in practice.

title: St John United Church of Christ - Worship 20 Sep 2020

classification.summary: A Sunday morning worship bulletin for St. John United Church of Christ, Melbourne, IA, for the Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost, September 20, 2020. The order of service draws on Psalm 145 and includes a call to worship, liturgical elements, and a reflection on wealth from Dickens's Great Expectations.

title: St John United Church of Christ - Worship 25 Oct 2020

classification.summary: A full Sunday worship bulletin for St. John United Church of Christ, Melbourne, IA on Reformation Sunday, October 25, 2020. The service centers on Matthew 22:36-39, the great commandment to love God and neighbor.

A Lessons and Carols service with reflections for Christmas Eve 2020, written by Tony E. Dillon Hansen. The liturgy weaves opening prayer, confession, and scripture readings through the trials of a difficult year toward the hope of the Incarnation.
